coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-assisted ratiometric colorimetric and fluorescent probe for triple-mode determination of nitrite based on MnO2 nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Bo
Wang, Chunjuan
He, Xueyan
Ma, Yongjun
Zhou, Min
Ma, Xin
Zhao, Shengguo
Fang, Yanjun
coredata.dc:description: A triple-mode colorimetric and fluorescent sensing scheme based on manganese dioxide nanoparticles (MnO2NPs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were developed to determine nitrite. MnO2NPs can oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into a blue oxidation product (TMBox), which is further oxidized into a yellow diimine derivative by nitrite. The ratio of absorbance at 652 nm to 452 nm was monitored as signal response for UV–vis detection mode. A “turn-off” CQDs fluorescence probe was also constructed for fluorescent detection mode. Smartphone tool kit was used to capture the color of sample for smartphone-based measurement. Various analytical performance under different detection modes were obtained and compared. The proposed methods were applied to food samples with satisfactory recoveries (83.3–106 %). The results were validated with AOAC standard spectrophotometric method. The current triple-mode detection were accurate, convenient, low-cost and fast for analyzing nitrite in foods and water samples on-site.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrite
Smartphone
Fluorescence
MnO2
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental condition of ratiometric colorimetric detection
Detection of nitrite in real samples with different detection modes

China (2021HK210), the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2018YFC1603700 ), the special fund from key topics (N0: AWS18J004 & N0: BWS17J025) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22166032, 21862019) for their financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis spectra of the MnO2NPs + TMB system with nitrite at different levels (The inset shows color change with different concentrations of nitrite). (B) Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs in the presence of different concentration of nitrite (The inset shows the fluorescence color change with nitrite at different levels under 365 nm UV lamp).
Schematic diagram of multi-mode determination of nitrite.
Comparison of analytical performance in different determination modes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by the 3σ/slope rule. Where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample solution.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The limit of quantitation (LOQ) was calculated by the 10σ/slope rule. Where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample solution.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Seven repeated determination (nitrite of 1.0 μg/mL) in the same day.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Seven repeated determination (nitrite of 1.0 μg/mL) on seven consecutive days.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Four repeated the establishment of calibration curves in the one month.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-assisted ratiometric colorimetric and fluorescent probe for triple-mode determination of nitrite based on MnO2 nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province
Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lanzhou Customs District P. R. China, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lanzhou Customs District P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Lanzhou Customs District P. R. China, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Military Medical Sciences Academy, Environmental and Operational Medicine Research Department, Tianjin 300050, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Environmental and Operational Medicine Research Department

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Military Medical Sciences Academy, Environmental and Operational Medicine Research Department, Tianjin 300050, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A triple-mode colorimetric and fluorescent sensing scheme based on manganese dioxide nanoparticles (MnO2NPs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were developed to determine nitrite. MnO2NPs can oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into a blue oxidation product (TMBox), which is further oxidized into a yellow diimine derivative by nitrite. The ratio of absorbance at 652 nm to 452 nm was monitored as signal response for UV–vis detection mode. A “turn-off” CQDs fluorescence probe was also constructed for fluorescent detection mode. Smartphone tool kit was used to capture the color of sample for smartphone-based measurement. Various analytical performance under different detection modes were obtained and compared. The proposed methods were applied to food samples with satisfactory recoveries (83.3–106 %). The results were validated with AOAC standard spectrophotometric method. The current triple-mode detection were accurate, convenient, low-cost and fast for analyzing nitrite in foods and water samples on-site.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is generally used as preservative and coloring agent in food processing, extensively exists in daily life. However, excessive nitrite intake will cause a series of adverse symptoms such as methemoglobinemia, cyanosis, headache, dizziness, rapid heart rate, nausea, even to death (Altunay, 2020). Thus, nitrite has been listed as highly toxic by the World Health Organization (WHO) because of its potential carcinogenicity. The Joint Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO) and WHO have set the maximum limit of nitrite in drinking water is 3.0 mg/L and a maximum daily intake of nitrite is 0.06 mg/kg in food (Alsaiari et al., 2021; Yilmaz, 2021). The maximum content of nitrite in milk power is restricted to 2.0 mg/kg and 0.1 mg/L in bottled water in China (CFDA, 2017). Therefore, it is important and imperative to establish a simple, rapid, accurate and user-friendly platforms for nitrite analysis in food and water resources.

Many instrumental methods such as spectrophotometry, ultra performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, fluorescence spectrometry, ion chromatography (IC), colorimetry and electrochemical methods, etc. have been developed for the determination of nitrite in aqueous solutions and foods. These methods can acquire reliable and accurate results in many severe measurement scenarios. HPLC and ion chromatography (Kim, Kim, Yang, & Sim, 2022; Lim et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2017) are typical standard methods for determining various anions including nitrite. However, most chromatography-based methods usually involve separation and purification process with an ion-exchange column and conductivity, electrochemical, UV absorption, or mass spectrometry (MS) detection (Rohrer, 2021). Sensitive and accurate determination of traces nitrite in samples with high salt content is still challenging (Ito, Takayama, Makabe, Mitsui, & Hirokawa, 2005; Nani Wang & Zhu, 2012) in many sample matrices considering that the use of high capacity column and suppression column and removal of interfering substances, like chloride ions (Kim et al., 2022) and extend the lifetime of analytical instruments (Lim et al., 2022), even with the help of a derivatization reaction (Akyüz, 2009) in GC–MS are inevitable. The determination procedures are complex and suffer problems with the stability and repeated treatment of column and high cost for operation and maintenance. Spectrophotometric method is relatively simple, efficient and cost-optimal method for nitrite monitoring owing to its stable operability mechanisms, which are related to the coupling reactions between nitrite and Griess reagent (sulfanilamide and N- (1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine) and other aromatic primary amines and clamping compounds (Polat, 2022). However, Most of Griess methods depend largely on changes of absorbance of a single wavelength induced by nitrite. The Griess methods possibly expose insufficient sensitivity for low-level analyte detection and are susceptible to interference in practical application situations (Wang, Liu, Zhu, Liu, & Niu, 2021). Recently, ratiometric colorimetric (Wang et al., 2021; Yilmaz, 2021; Yoon et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021) based on dual wavelength was used for the detection of nitrite. In comparison with single wavelength spectrophotometry, ratiometric colorimetric strategy can enhance the accuracy and reproducibility (Yilmaz, 2021), sensitivity and better anti-interference ability (Wang et al., 2021).

Manganese dioxide nanomaterials have been employed as excellent alternative to natural enzymes due to its excellent oxidase–like activity and various attractive physical and chemical merits (Qian, Tan, Zhang, Leng, & Chen, 2022). In the absence of H2O2, manganese dioxide nanoparticles can easily react with some chromogenic substrates like 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to produce a colored oxidized product for spectrophotometry (Ma, Nishina, & Bianco, 2021) and smartphone-based colorimetry (Li et al., 2019). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is another colorimetric and fluorescent probe widely studied carbon-based zero dimensional nanomaterial with extensive photonic and electronic properties (Yang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022). Highly selective fluorescence methods based on CQDs have been applied for the detection of nitrite (Rong et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2022). CQDs-based fluorescent probe dealt with the fluorescence quenching procedures caused by the diazotization reaction between aromatic amine on the surface of CQDs and nitrite (Yu et al., 2021; Yumin Hao et al., 2022). Fluorescent probes based on Inner filter effect (IFE) (Wu et al., 2020; Zhu, Shen, Hu, Yang, & Jiang, 2022) would be readily quenched by matching the emission wavelength of fluorophore and optical absorption of the chromophore resulting in change of absorption. Constructing fluorescent probe through IFE enable fluorescence and spectrophotometry to achieve double-mode detections (Das, Biswas, Bhattacharya, & Nath, 2022; Wu et al., 2020), which can improve the sensitivity and reliability of determination. The strategies combined MnO2 nano-material and CQDs have been widely reported for visual drug loading (Gong et al., 2020), intracellular GSH (Deng et al., 2020) imaging. The fluorescent and colorimetric probe based on ratiometric detection made the method better anti-interference capability and suitable for quantitative measurements in biological matrices.

In this study, a triple-mode nitrite assay based on MnO2NPs and CQDs sensors was successfully developed. Mimetic enzyme MnO2NPs directly oxidized TMB into blue oxidization product TMBox (λmax = 652 nm). Nitrite further oxidized TMBox to yield the yellow diimine-derived product (λmax = 452 nm). The UV absorption intensity decreased at 652 nm and increased at 452 nm in the presence of nitrite so that a ratiometric colorimetric probe for nitrite was realized. In addition, yellow diimine derivatives can quench the fluorescence of CQDs based on the IFE mechanism (Carstea, Mounier, Redon, & Popa, 2020). Therefore, a simple fluorescence “on–off” sensor for nitrite detection was constructed with the lower detection limit. Meanwhile, smartphone colorimetry was conveniently implemented in the visual and quantitative detection. The current triple-mode detecting strategy provides more possibilities for nitrite evaluation in food products under different scenarios.

3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Manganese chloride tetrahydrate was obtained from Tianjin Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Citric acid and acrylamide were purchased from Aladdin. Stock standard solution of nitrite (1000 μg/mL) was supplied by Beijing Northern Weiye Metrology Technology Research Institute. All regents were used as received without further purification unless otherwise specified. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ*cm) was provided by a Millipore water purification system. A description of the instruments used in the experiments has been provided in the Electronic Supporting Material (ESM).

MnO2NPs were synthesized according to the reference method (Ma et al., 2021). 480 μL of MnCl2 (1 mol/L) and 1200 μL of NaOH (1 mol/L) aqueous solution were successively added into 20 mL of BSA aqueous solution (20 mg/mL) under stirring, and the mixture was stirred for 3 h. Then, the brown product MnO2NPs were dialyzed for 12 h using a dialysis membrane (MW = 3.5 kDa).

CQDs were synthesized according to the reference (Li, Liu, Sun, Pan, & Wang, 2017) with a minor modification. Specifically, 0.25 g citric acid and 0.5 g acrylamide were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL sealed polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave, heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the light yellow solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and then dialyzed for 24 h.

The feasibility of ratiometric colorimetric detection of nitrite was verified by recording the response of nitrite at various concentrations. First, TMB (300 μL, 0.4 mM) and MnO2NPs (150 μL, 0.5 mg/mL) were sequentially added to NaAc-HAc buffer solution (1490 μL, 0.1 mol/L, pH = 4.0). After reacting for 10 min at room temperature, different concentrations of nitrite solution were added to the above mixtures. The mixture solutions were incubated at 37 °C for 20 min. Finally, the UV–vis absorption signal at 652 nm and 452 nm were recorded respectively. The ratio of A452 to A652 was calculated and employed as analytical signal response for nitrite detection.

TMB (80 μL, 1.0 mM) and MnO2NPs (50 μL, 1.0 mg/mL) were sequentially added to NaAc-HAc buffer solution (1810 μL, 0.1 mol/L, pH = 3.5). After reacting for 10 min at room temperature, different concentrations of nitrite solution were added to the above mixtures. The mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for 20 min. Then, 20 μL of CQDs was added and incubated for 10 min. Fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded in the 350–550 nm range under excitation at 350 nm.

Two self-made smartphone tool boxes (Peng et al., 2022) were used for smartphone-based colorimetric measurement of nitrite. The color of reaction solution were respectively captured and determined by PhotoMetrix (Helfer et al., 2017) using an Android smartphone. The built-in stoichiometry of PhotoMetrix directly gave determination results without additional data processing.
Tap water and Yellow River water were filtered through a 0.22 μm pore size filter membrane, and the filtrate were collected for further measurement, respectively.

All food samples were purchased from local supermarket in Lanzhou, China. Sausage, ham, canned lunch meat, sauced pickles were accurately weighed (5.0 g), crushed with a masher, placed in a 100 mL conical flask, and added to 30 mL of water respectively. Sauced pickles was first decolorized by activated carbon. After ultrasonic treatment for 30 min and heating in a water bath at 75 °C for 5 min, the samples solution was cooled down to room temperature. Next, 15 mL of ethanol was added and vortexed 2 min. The solution was transferred into a 50 mL volumetric flask and made up to the mark with ultrapure water and let stand. Finally, the solution was centrifuged and filtered, the supernatant was collected for nitrite detection.

All experiments were carried out three times and the data was expressed as mean ± SD (standard deviation). The student’s t-test was used for the statistical evaluation of the results. The acquired spectra were exported as xls or txt files, replotted by Origin Pro 7.5. Treatment of data and statistical analysis (average value and recovery) were performed using datasheets prepared in Microsoft Excel 2013.

In the pH = 3–5, TMB can be oxidized to blue oxidation complex (TMBox) with an absorption at 652 nm by MnO2NPs due to its oxidase–like property. The blue TMBox is further oxidized by nitrite to produce a yellow diimine derivative which has a new absorption signal at 452 nm. The absorbance of two wavelengths exists a relation of “as one falls, another rises”. The color variation of reacting solution is observed from blue, blue-green to yellow with increasing the concentration of nitrite (Fig. 1 A). The UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB, MnO2NPs and MnO2NPs-TMB were showed in Fig. S4A and B, respectively.

The catalytic kinetics mechanism of MnO2NPs-TMB reaction was investigated by varying concentrations of TMB at a fixed concentration of MnO2NPs (Fig. S5A). The Michaelis–Menten constant (km) was calculated according to the Lineweaver–Burk double reciprocal plot (Shi, Fan, Ai, & Zhu, 2015):
(1)
1 / v
=
k m
/
v max
/
1 /
s
+
1 /
v max
where ν is initial velocity, [s] is the concentration of substrate and ν max is the maximal reaction velocity. km measured the ability of enzyme to bind substrate and was calculated to be 0.027 mM in this study (Fig. S5B), which was lower than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) (Meng Liu, Chen, Hongtao, & Quan, 2012). The result indicates that MnO2NPs possesses a good oxidase–like activity.

Although MnO2NPs can quench the fluorescence of CQDs, it was first reduced by TMB in this experiment, which prevented the quenching procedure. Then, the diimine derivative produced by addition of nitrite quenched the fluorescence of the CQDs. The quenching mechanism was investigated below. The fluorescent emission spectra of CQDs and the UV–vis absorption spectra of diimine derivative were recorded. Fig. S6 showed that CQDs exhibited blue fluorescence at 418 nm under excitation of 350 nm. In Fig. S7A, the UV–vis absorption spectra of diimine derivative and the fluorescence spectra of CQDs overlapped considerably, indicating that the fluorescence of CQDs would be quenched by diimine derivative according to both Förster (Fluorescence) resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Kikuchi, Takakusa, & Nagano, 2004) or IFE. In order to verify the conclusion, each fluorescence intensity value of CQDs were corrected for possible IFE in presence of nitrite (indirect effect) according to Eq (2) (El-Shaheny, Yoshida, & Fuchigami, 2020).
(2)
F
corr
=
F
obs
×
a n t i log
A
ex
+
A
em
/ 2
where Fobs represents the observed fluorescence intensity, Fcorr represents the corrected fluorescence intensity after omitting IFE from Fobs. Aex and Aem represent the absorbance values of the quencher at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the fluorophore, respectively. The inhibition efficiency (%E) of corrected and observed fluorescence was calculated via Eq (3).
(3)
% E
=
1
-
F /
F 0
×
100

The relationship between %E (The observed and corrected fluorescence of CQDs quenched) and the concentration of nitrite (Fig. S7B) deduced that IFE mainly participated in fluorescence quenching by approximately 35 % of the total inhibition efficiency for yellow diamine derivative. Another evidence was that IFE became more remarkable at high concentration of nitrite and might seriously distort the fluorescence spectra (Li & Hu, 2007), simultaneously the blue shift of fluorescence peak gradually occurred (Carstea et al., 2020) (Fig. 1B).

Stern–Volmer mechanistic study was conducted at 20–45 °C according to the Stern–Volmer equation (El-Shaheny et al., 2020).
(4)
F 0
/ F
=
1
+
K D
Q
where KD is the Stern–Volmer dynamic quenching constant and [Q] is the molar concentration of quencher. The slope of Stern–Volmer diagram (KD) value was found to increase obviously with increasing the temperature (Fig. S7D). The behavior was attributed to dynamic quenching mechanism because the higher temperature resulted in the faster diffusion and greater collisional quenching (El-Shaheny et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. S7C, the UV–vis absorption spectra of each combination were exhibited. The new absorption peak did not appear in the presence of CQDs. The phenomenon suggested that new compounds didn’t formed and there was a weak interaction between diamine derivative and CQDs. Therefore, it can be conclude that the quenching process was dominated by dynamic quenching (Feng et al., 2021). The principle of triple-mode detection of nitrite is displayed in Scheme1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental condition of ratiometric colorimetric detection
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal reaction conditions including the concentration of MnO2NPs, pH and incubation time were studied. As shown in Fig. S8A, the absorbance value at 652 nm of TMBox gradually increased with pH from 2 to 3, the value kept roughly stable in the pH = 3–5, and decreased when the pH was above 5. Therefore, the final pH was chosen to be 4.0.

The time-dependent absorbance change (Fig. S8B) indicated that the absorbance at 652 nm maintained a stable trend after reaction for 10 min. Thus, a reaction time of 10 min was utilized in order to ensure that TMB was catalytically oxidized sufficiently.

With the various concentration of MnO2NPs from 7.5 to 45 μg/mL, the absorbance at 652 nm gradually stabilized when the concentration was greater than 37.5 μg/mL (Fig. S8C), indicating that TMB was completely oxidized. Therefore, 38 μg/mL was selected as the optimal amount of MnO2NPs for UV–vis detection mode.

The oxidation of TMBox with the addition of nitrite was further investigated. With the increasing of nitrite concentration, the absorption of TMBox at 652 nm decreased because TMBox was oxidized by nitrite, while the absorption at 452 nm attributing to diamine derivative gradually increased. The ratio of absorbance 452 nm to 652 nm (A452/A652) presented an increasing trend with the increasing of nitrite concentration. The value of A452/A652 reached the maximum at pH = 4.0 (Fig. S9A). Consequently, pH = 4.0 of HAc-NaAc solution was selected to be buffer.

The temperature dependence in the presence nitrite was tested in the range of 20 –45 °C. The ratio value of A452/A652 increased as the increasing temperature (Fig. S9B). The result showed that the high temperature was beneficial for the continuous oxidation of TMBox in the presence nitrite. Fig. S9C exhibited that the absorbance at 652 nm was relatively stable from 20 to 35 °C, and gradually decreased above 40 °C. Therefore, in order to avoid the disadvantageous influence of temperature on TMBox, 37 °C was finally employed as the oxidation reaction temperature for TMBox.

The variation of A452/A652 was observed within 40 min at 37 °C (Fig. S9D). The ratio value reached equilibrium when reaction time exceeded 20 min, indicating that 20 min was enough to completely reaction.

According to Fig. S10A, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs acquired a minimum at pH = 3.5, indicating that the quenching process presumably occurred at a pH below 3.5. Therefore, pH = 3.5 was selected in the fluorescent detecting mode.

The fluorescent intensity of CQDs gradually decreased with increasing the concentration of MnO2NPs in the 1.25–30 μg/mL range. The fluorescence intensity reached minimum and kept stable above 25 μg/mL (Fig. S10B), indicating that the fluorescence of CQDs was completely quenched with such concentration. The final concentration of MnO2NPs was decided to be 30 μg/mL to ensure efficient quenching.

The fluorescence intensity of CQDs was observed at different temperature within 60 min (Fig. S10C). The fluorescence was stable when CQDs was quenched at 20–30 °C. The quenching degree of CQDs became greater with increasing temperature in same quenching time because the higher temperature led to the faster diffusion and collision during quenching process. However, the fluorescence intensity tended to slightly increase over time at the higher temperature. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of CQDs was determined in the temperature range 20–30 °C. The TMBox-oxidized and CQDs mixtures were incubated at the room temperature for 10 min prior to fluorescent detection.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, nitrite was determined to assess the analytical performance using different detecting modes. For UV–vis mode, the linear plots of A452 nm, A652 nm and A452/A652 versus NO2 − concentrations were given respectively (Fig. S11). The ratiometric absorbance and the concentration of NO2 − exhibited a good liner relationship (A452/A652 = 0.0812C + 0.4775, R2 = 0.9967) in the range of 0.15–12 μg/mL. The comparison of three standard curves in the respective detection mode in Fig. S11 indicated that the ratiometric absorbance had a higher sensitivity and a better linearity than that of single wavelength absorbance.

Under 365 nm UV light, the color of fluorescence gradually weakened from deep blue with increasing NO2 −
concentrations and the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs decreased accordingly. (Fig. 1B). (F0 – F) and
NO2 − concentration showed a good linear relationship with the linear regression equation (F0 – F = 35.5C + 88.4, R2 = 0.9971) in the range of 0.05–20 μg/mL. F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of nitrite, respectively.

As shown in Fig. S12, colorimetric determination of nitrite was conducted based on sharp color variation with smartphone. An Android APP Photometrix univariate analysis module (Helfer, 2017) was used to perform the calibration and sampling. The B channel value in RGB model and nitrite concentrations exhibited decent linear relationship (B = 6.046C + 102.9, R2 = 0.9960) for visible light colorimetric mode. The H channel value in HSV model and nitrite concentrations displayed a good linear relationship (H = -0.632C + 57.20, R2 = 0.9890) for fluorescent colorimetric mode.

The obtained analytical features of triple-mode detection of nitrite, including linear range, correlation coefficients, LOD (3σ / S), LOQ (10σ / S) and precision were collected in Table 1 . The precision (intra-and inter-day) of the proposed methods was evaluated by analyzing nitrite concentration of 1.0 μg/mL seven times in the same day and on seven consecutive days. The intra-day and inter-day precision were in the ranges of 1.6–2.9 % and 2.2–3.9 % respectively. As shown in Table S1, the current method was compared with the sensing system previously reported.

Compared with fluorescence detection mode, standard UV–vis detection had the higher stability according to relative standard deviation of slope of calibration curves although the linear range was narrow. Fluorescence-based detection expectedly gained a lower LOD and a wider linear range. The poor precision with fluorescence method probably due to the fact that the fluorescence was always affected by temperature and other external conditions. For smartphone-based colorimetry, calibration curves of standard solution and analytical results of samples were directly obtained by PhotoMetrix, which provided advantages in field portability, cost efficiency and user friendliness. The three-mode analysis developed in current work provides highly reliable results for different detection scenarios of nitrite.

In order to assess anti-interference ability of the method for standard UV–vis detection mode, different cations, anions and small organic molecules were evaluated by adding a certain amount of each substance to the solution containing 0.42 µg/mL NO2 − under the same experimental conditions. The tolerance limit of various ions was defined as the largest amount making a variation of less than ± 5 %. The results were listed in Table 2 . It can be seen the most cations, anions, such as high concentration of NaCl, and small organic molecules exhibited high tolerance limits, indicating that the method had very high selectivity toward nitrite. Exceeding 80 times interference from Fe3+ and 100 times interference from I−, Hg2+ might result from the slight oxidation capacity of Hg2+, Fe3+ and the reduction capacity of I−. However, under such matrix circumstance, F− and EDTA can be used as masking agent for Fe3+. Hg2+ can be masked by thiourea. Considering the tolerance limits of I− and Cu2+ is twice, I− can be first removed by adding moderate amount of Cu2+. These results indicated that the UV–vis mode detection had very high selectivity toward NO2 −.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nitrite in real samples with different detection modes
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed multi-mode analytical methods were applied to analyze nitrite concentrations in different water and food samples. As shown in Table 3 , Table S2, Table S3, different concentrations of nitrite were spiked into the samples for the recovery experiments. The acceptable recoveries from 83.3 % to 106 % were obtained, indicating that the nitrite can be accurately analyzed in practical simples.

The accuracy and reliability of ratiometric colorimetric detection mode were confirmed by a reference method based on spectrophotometry (Association of Official Analytical Chemists (AOAC), 2016). Table 4 presented the
NO
2
-
content measured in canned luncheon meat, ham, sausage and pickle. Water sample Certified Reference Materials (CRM) (containing F−, Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 −,
SO
4
2-
,
PO
4
3-
, Br−) was used to verify the accuracy. The results indicated that at a 95 % confidence level, there was no significant difference between the proposed method and spectrophotometric method using the Student t-test. The current method was suitable for the routine quantitative analysis of nitrite in the practical sample with high accuracy and reliability.

Simple and accurate estimating of nitrite levels in food and water bodies is very important to monitor food safety and water quality. We have successfully developed for triple-mode detection of nitrite based on MnO2NPs and CQDs. Compared with conventional single mode methods, ratiometric colorimetric method exhibited enough selectivity, sensitivity and good liner range. The standard fluorescence method achieved a lower LOD and a wider linear range. Fluorescence quenching mechanism of CQDs was confirmed as a combination of IFE and dynamic quenching. Smartphone-based colorimetry was convenient and fast to assess nitrite levels by Android smartphone without additional data processing. The triple-mode detection performed well for the measurement of high-salt samples due to its high selectivity and excellent anti-interference ability. Compared with the methods that require less salt in the samples, the triple-mode detection can avoid the use of sample pretreatment columns and reduce the procedure and cost. The present method is specially promising and valued for accurate nitrite monitoring.

Bo Peng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Chunjuan Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Xueyan He: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yongjun Ma: Data curation, Validation. Min Zhou: Software. Xin Ma: Validation, Data curation. Shengguo Zhao: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yanjun Fang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Research Projects of General Administration of Customs of the P. R. China (2021HK210), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1603700), the special fund from key topics (N0: AWS18J004 & N0: BWS17J025) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22166032, 21862019) for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.135151.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
